item.title: The best of all possible multiverses! (+200 upvotes)
item.content:encoded: Glad to see this is still creeping upwards! Godspeed, my unstoppable self-insert world-ender!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was shallow. Not a mainlist material in my opinion. If it was a Joke, it would be ok. It is a cohesive narrative, it have its good points, it can have some more lols in it and be fun without having to lose all its relatively serious tone. This is actually one of the kinds of jokes I like most.

But as a mainlister I saw nothing for it. It is way worse than the Black Queen, and I never liked her as a concept, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As it seems, a K-Class scenario will be unleashed if any single Lloyd dies. If the other Lloyds die at the same time or not, it was not described. Being all linked by the shared anomalous status, the Lloyds themselves can be exempted from the norm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is pretty much a lot of what I thought. The story is coherent, but emotionally void. It adds nothing to the Foundation, on the contrary. At most it makes its "Main Foundation" into some sort of quasi-hero for keeping up their Lloyd, but that was too shallow an execution for this concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but given the risk of causing the death of every other human in the universe at the same time, are you going to be the one to sign off that particular experiment? Far safer to just keep the guy alive by every available method.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, so, does the existence of at least one other Lloyd in a universe mean that any of the Lloyds can die without the rest of humanity being extinguished first? Like, the effect only manifests if there's only one Lloyd in a particular universe at the given time? I mean, I would expect that to be the case, otherwise they'd all have to die simultaneously… though that's not outside the realm of an SCP of course.

Also, can't they use one of their various time-fuckery objects to copy That Other Universe where Lloyd was erased from history before even being conceived, then set a rule that the anomaly used to carry this out must itself never be retroactively destroyed, unless the method used to do that is itself first used to preemptively erase Lloyd even earlier in the timeline?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you suggesting replacing Lloyd with Dr. Bright? Depending on how the anomaly works, that could be considered the same as killing Lloyd, thus triggering SCP-3856. But it could work. Maybe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where's 963 when you need it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just amazing. Infinite Foundations freaking out over this problem is a sight to imagine, and the final stinger made me laugh out loud. Solid +1, please continue with time/space/universe shenanigans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"They" technically is the third-person, gender neutral pronoun you are looking for. It has historical precedence. But so too does "he". However, that's only in regards to people, because "it" is considered to be dehumanizing.

All the more reason to use it! SCPs are not supposed to be referred to in their documentation by gendered pronouns because, from a logistical standpoint, they aren't people to the Foundation. At the very least, it makes the Ethics Committee feel less terrible before they opiod themselves to sleep at night.

item.title: The best of all possible timelines! (+100 upvotes)
item.content:encoded:
I did it! We did it! Hooray!

Thanks to everyone who voted on this, left feedback, helped critique, or just didn't stop me plugging this relentlessly in chat (as I am wont to do for anything at +90 or above). Sorry if it got annoying, and thanks for not murdering me. I really appreciate everything everyone on this site does, and it makes me unbelievably happy to see other people like this article as much as I do. You guys are great :)

Also, for symmetry's sake, I just want to note that SCP-2856 was my first +100, and this (3856) is my second. Looks like I may have to wait until Series V for my next milestone :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The obvious answer to that is to gather up a few billion Researcher Lloyds and strand 'em in some universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lloydception!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But what happens if one Lloyd tries to kill another Lloyd?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't care how much of a pain this causes the Foundation, as you freely establish in the article that the Foundation can just share Lloyds with different universes and the whole thing feels utterly meaningless. Not that I even was able to buy into this idea on a base level in the first place.

Why should I care about the pain this brings the Foundation? Because it's the Foundation? If Lloyd dies early, a K-class scenario occurs really quickly, but any kind of nuance in this or exploration into how fucked up this shit can get doesn't even really happen because it just passes itself off real easily as "a thing that happens in a universe". I don't get a sense of how the Foundation feels even on a real clinical level, I don't get a sense of how Lloyd feels, I don't understand the need for multi-universal stuff when it lessens any impact you go for, I can't simply care at all.

SCP-2003 did this kind of thing really fucking well, and it was even more clinical and dry. I got an actual sense of how the Foundation felt through the usage of exploring alternate universes while weighing the cost of doing so against it and trying to maintain a sense of normalcy. And the end-of-universe situations presented and that felt a lot more impactful and hard-hitting than any of the ones here.

SCP-1739 did the "ever-imminent mortality" thing too, and it also had other universes involved in these shenanigans, but it did so a lot more striking and emotional. Which, it's fine if you don't want to go that route, but you have to really put in the work to show me why, from a clinical perspective, I need to feel for the Foundation here. The previous two articles mentioned showed the inhuman and human side of things in a very real way; this just feels like a lot of cheap narrative to make Lloyd feel important. He may be important in-universe, but certainly not how I feel when reading this. Your tale about him literally does what you set out to do with this in a stronger way, and I read that before I was even aware of this article. Even though I don't think that did it quite as well as I'd like to see, it at least did it for me.

And on a more personal note, I absolutely feel weary about alternate universe shenanigans by now. Time paradox fuckery is fun because that adds a deeper layer to the equation, but when both the problem and the solution is "another universe", it just makes me feel like I can't be bothered because it allows for a number of easy ways to write stuff. Which is fine, if the easy writing still results in a strong positive impression. And I feel the opposite here, like my time was just wasted by the end of it all. Really strong words, but it's absolutely how I feel about it. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: that's a pretty reasonable containment procedure, given that disregarding it causes the destruction of the entire universe, so "kill on sight" looks about right to me

item.title: Re: Footnote 1
item.content:encoded:
Aw, I thought I might have saved the multiverse. Foiled again! I'll get you next time, Researcher Lloyd!

NEEEEXT TIIIIIIIIIIME!!!

item.title: Re: Footnote 1
item.content:encoded: Ack, sorry, I thought I'd posted here but I guess either I misremembered or wikidot bugged out. I actually added that footnote in response to your comment — it was a minor oversight I didn't address in the original drafts, and was completely my fault. Sorry about that :/

item.title: Footnote 1
item.content:encoded:
They've tried that - it's actually the first footnote:

Dependent on his existence as an individual, though not his continued employment by the Foundation. Removing Researcher Lloyd from his post as Foundation staff has thus far failed to avert the associated apocalyptic event.

Maybe if they went back & unmade the Foundation itself, but that's just asking for a whole mess of other end-of-the-world scenarios being made possible…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Have they tried firing him?

Every mention of Researcher Lloyd seems very adamant about stating that he is a Foundation employee, a lot of the article seems to imply that if there is a Foundation and there is a Lloyd then Lloyd will be a Foundation employee, and all I can wonder is, what if he wasn't anymore?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Theme music?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alternate title suggestion: "Interdimensional Hot Potato".

So you can stick a bunch of Lloyds on a populated world and they'd only trigger a localized K-class, hence not every Lloyd universe is necessarily doomed. In which case I'd repeat the dumping strategy on some sacrificial world, except send over a Lloyd with a chest bomb the moment they start getting spiteful. Or have the multi-foundation gang up on any organization in that sacrificial world that's aware of Lloyd, THEN start dumping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The word “it”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Different tastes, I suppose. This isn't going anywhere so just consider me the fly in this SCP's proverbial punch bowl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amazing idea, Amazing execution. I love meta scps, and interuniversal scps. The punchline was great too. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: Damn you, wikidot

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, I'll clarify that — it was only supposed to refer to the instance being talked about, rather than multiple instances. Curse English, and its lack of a third-person non-gender-specific singular pronoun! I'm apparently an idiot who doesn't know about the word "it". That'll teach me to comment when I'm tired.

The wording's been clarified a little in any case, and I've moved some information into footnotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty cool, but I'm a little confused in a few places:

Should the probability of SCP-3856-1's imminent death rise above 0.01% at any time, they are to be immediately ejected to the nearest inhabited universe.

The full range of SCP-3856's effects are not known, but the primary element appears to be the fact that Researcher Lloyd will inevitably (100% chance, no observed margin of error) cease brain activity only following the elimination of every other extant human (defined as a non-anomalous instance of homo sapiens, native to the relevant universe) within the universe in which they currently reside.

Addendum.1: Event 3856-Alpha: On ██/██/████, despite agreed-upon travel protocols, a single instance of Researcher Lloyd manifested within Site-54, equipped with standard Foundation provisions and equipment (circa 1880 AD). They were later determined to have originated from U-4046Y

What are "they"? Does "they" refer to multiple instances of Researcher Lloyd?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I like this work more because it doesn't do all that. The core of the story is the pain in the Foundations' ass that Lloyd causes, and derailing that plotline to listen to how sad Lloyd feels would be trite IMO, as well as detrimental to the flow of the work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I adore this SCP. This is incredibly well written and I find it really compelling. my +1 is not even in question.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is the most I've been torn on an SCP, ever.

I really, really wanted to like this. The idea of a man always being forced to be the last person alive in the universe is both ambitious and great fodder for pathos. Your efforts to describe in-detail the Foundation's relationship with other universes is very interesting, if a bit contrived.

However, I find myself compelled to -1 for the lack of the aforementioned pathos. Your article is about one of the most horrifying things that can happen to a person and yet, fundamentally, lacks humanity. How does Lloyd feel about this? What is it like to experience this over and over again? These were questions I hoped would be addressed, but never were.

This is a technically solid and intellectually fascinating work, but I require humanoid SCPs to emotionally engage me, and this just didn't at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, there's always a loophole. :( My novote's more for the first point, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To answer point 2, the article states…

To clarify: while a human population of several billion will result only in the worldwide extermination of said humans, the death of Researcher Lloyd within an uninhabited or sparsely populated universe risks a high-level Yggdrasil Severance Event, and the subsequent collapse of adjacent dimensional regions.

It happens to also be a bit of a shout-out to my SCP, as "Yggdrasil-Severance Event" is shorthand for "the universe's natural laws go haywire, possibly destroys the universe". It also mentions that it will collapse other "nearby" universes as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My thoughts are two:

1) The containment procedures are overdramatic, with the whole "If you dare harm him, you shall be terminated! Do not touch, look at, enter the containment chamber of or even think about this anomaly, this means you!"

2) If they can access other universes, why not find one already stripped of human life — maybe even one a Lloyd has nuked — and stick all the Lloyds there? They'll never die!

…Though I kind of feel like that suggestion requires the "guy pointing at head" meme image. :/ It might not be a good idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the contrary, I think that the ending line is great. I did know it was coming, though, seeing as I have known of this idea for months now and that line was in the original idea. I think it is charming in all the right ways.

And that previous sentence can be extended to the entirety of this article as well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is Rick and Morty-esque in the best way possible. I really love everything about this except for the final stinger, which crossed the line to feeling too Rick-and-Morty for my taste. Still, enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am really confused by this

Yet I love it

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The next in a series where I confuse everybody, in-universe and out, it's a scip about the last man alive! Hope you enjoy reading it as much as I enjoyed writing it — multiuniversal stuff is always a blast, and multiuniversal diplomacy doubly so.

Attributions:

  • Multiple people who critiqued this as a draft. Mortos, TheBillith, Tuomey Tombstone, Phantom Ink, DrChandra, and Varaxous, are those I have a record of, but I'm sure there were others. Just PM me and I'll add you.
  • 9Volt, for inspiring my multiversal shenanigans with a scip of their own, unfortunately taken down for re-tooling.
  • CadaverCommander, for coming up with the title and encouraging me to post.

Image Credit: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:FBI_Laboratory_scientist.jpg

Read more about Lloyd's escapades here, or check out my author page for vague writing insights, pointless lists, and links to more of my stuff.
